Antibiotic resistance
Antimicrobial resistance
Multidrug resistance
Bacterial evolution
triples.object: Bacterial evolution may refer to the biological evolution of bacteria as studied in:

triples.subject: Pathogen
Bacterial infection
triples.object: Pathogenic bacteria are bacteria that can cause disease . [ 1 ] This article focuses on the bacteria that are pathogenic to humans. Most species of bacteria are harmless and many are beneficial but others can cause infectious diseases . The number of these pathogenic species in humans is estimated t

triples.subject: Antimicrobial stewardship
triples.object: Antimicrobial stewardship ( AMS ) refers to coordinated efforts to promote the optimal use of antimicrobial agents, including drug choice, dose, route of administration, and duration of therapy. [ 1 ] [ 2 ]

triples.subject: Nosocomial infection
Infection control
Antiseptic
Disinfectant
Public health interventions
triples.object: A public health intervention is any effort, policy, or program intended to improve mental and physical health at the population level . Interventions involve social movements that strive to support public health at different levels of society. [ 1 ] Public health interventions may be run by a variet

triples.subject: Antibiotic misuse
Overuse of antibiotics
Resistance to antiviral drugs
triples.object: Drug resistance is the reduction in effectiveness of a medication such as an antimicrobial or an antineoplastic in treating a disease or condition. [ 1 ] The term is used in the context of resistance that pathogens or cancers have "acquired", that is, resistance has evolved. Antimicrobial resistance

triples.subject: Bioterrorism
Biodefense
Pandemic
Antibiotic resistance in gonorrhea
triples.object: Neisseria gonorrhoeae , the bacterium that causes the sexually transmitted infection gonorrhea , has developed antibiotic resistance to many antibiotics. The bacteria was first identified in 1879. [ 1 ]

triples.subject: Resistome
triples.object: The resistome has been used to describe to two similar yet separate concepts:

triples.subject: Antibiotic use in the United States poultry farming industry
triples.object: Antibiotic use in the United States poultry farming industry is the controversial prophylactic use of antibiotics in the country's poultry farming industry. It differs from the common practice in Europe, where antibiotics for growth promotion were disallowed in the 1950s.

triples.subject: Cross-resistance
triples.object: Cross-resistance is when something develops resistance to several substances that have a similar mechanism of action . For example, if a certain type of bacteria develops antimicrobial resistance to one antibiotic , that bacteria will also have resistance to several other antibiotics that target the

triples.subject: Comprehensive Antibiotic Resistance Database
triples.object: The Comprehensive Antibiotic Resistance Database (CARD) is a biological database that collects and organizes reference information on antimicrobial resistance genes, proteins and phenotypes. [ 1 ] [ 2 ] The database covers all types of drug classes and resistance mechanisms and structures its data b

triples.subject: ESKAPE
triples.object: ESKAPE is an acronym comprising the scientific names of six highly virulent and antibiotic resistant bacterial pathogens including: Enterococcus faecium , Staphylococcus aureus , Klebsiella pneumoniae , Acinetobacter baumannii , Pseudomonas aeruginosa , and Enterobacter spp. [ 1 ] The acronym is som

triples.subject: Drug resistance
triples.object: Drug resistance is the reduction in effectiveness of a medication such as an antimicrobial or an antineoplastic in treating a disease or condition. [ 1 ] The term is used in the context of resistance that pathogens or cancers have "acquired", that is, resistance has evolved. Antimicrobial resistance

triples.subject: Multidrug-resistant bacteria
triples.object: Multidrug-resistant ( MDR ) bacteria are bacteria that are resistant to three or more classes of antimicrobial drugs, [ 1 ] making them hard to treat. [ 2 ] MDR bacteria have seen an increase in prevalence in recent years [ 3 ] and pose serious risks to public health . [ 4 ] Previously, MDR bacteria

triples.subject: Antimicrobial resistance in Australia
triples.object: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) directly kills about 1,600 people each year in Australia. [ 1 ] This is a currently serious threat to both humans and animals in the country. [ 2 ] Antimicrobial resistance occurs when a microorganism (i.e. fungi , bacteria , viruses ) evolves and gains the ability to

triples.subject: Tetracycline antibiotics
triples.object: Tetracyclines are a group of broad-spectrum antibiotic compounds that have a common basic structure and are either isolated directly from several species of Streptomyces bacteria or produced semi-synthetically from those isolated compounds. [ 1 ] Tetracycline molecules comprise a linear fused tetrac

triples.subject: Repository of Antibiotic resistance Cassettes
triples.object: RAC otherwise known as R epository of A ntibiotic resistance C assettes is a database that uses the automatic Attacca annotation system in order to comprehensively annotate gene-cassettes and transposable elements in a stream-lined manner and to discover novel gene cassettes. [ 1 ] Antibiotic resist

triples.subject: Antibiotic synergy
triples.object: Antibiotic synergy is one of three responses possible when two or more antibiotics are used simultaneously to treat an infection. In the synergistic response, the applied antibiotics work together to produce an effect more potent than if each antibiotic were applied singly. [ 1 ] Compare to the addi

triples.subject: Multidrug-resistant Gram-negative bacteria
triples.object: Multidrug resistant Gram-negative bacteria ( MDRGN bacteria ) are a type of Gram-negative bacteria with resistance to multiple antibiotics .  They can cause bacteria infections that pose a serious and rapidly emerging threat for hospitalized patients and especially patients in intensive care units .

triples.subject: New Delhi metallo-beta-lactamase 1
triples.object: NDM-1 [ 1 ] is an enzyme in some strains of bacteria that confers resistance to a broad range of beta-lactam antibiotics . These include the antibiotics of the carbapenem family, which are a mainstay for the treatment of antibiotic-resistant bacterial infections. The gene for NDM-1 is one member of

triples.subject: Fabimycin
triples.object: Fabimycin is an newly developed antibiotic candidate which is effective against gram-negative bacterias , an unusually problematic class of bacteria that uses thicker cell walls and molecular efflux pumps to protect themselves by preventing the antibiotics reaching inside the cells. [ 1 ] [ 2 ]

triples.subject: Multidrug resistance pump
triples.object: A multidrug resistance (MDR) pump , or multidrug efflux pump , is any efflux pump which is able to expel multiple different categories of antimicrobial drugs. Multidrug resistance pumps can thus confer multiple drug resistance to a microbe and are of interest to the field of microbiology as one of t

triples.subject: Laura Piddock
triples.object: Laura Piddock is a microbiologist, specialising in antibiotics and antibiotic resistance in bacteria. She is Professor Emerita at the University of Birmingham , UK and also Scientific Director within the Global Antibiotic Research and Development Partnership. [ 1 ]

triples.subject: Global Leaders Group on Antimicrobial Resistance
triples.object: The Global Leaders Group on Antimicrobial Resistance consists of world leaders and experts from across sectors working together to accelerate political action on antimicrobial resistance (AMR) . [ 1 ] [ 2 ] [ 3 ]

triples.subject: Antimicrobials in aquaculture
triples.object: Antimicrobials destroy bacteria , viruses , fungi , algae , and other microbes . The cells of bacteria ( prokaryotes ), such as salmonella , differ from those of higher-level organisms ( eukaryotes ), such as fish. Antibiotics are chemicals designed to either kill or inhibit the growth of pathogenic

triples.subject: Antimicrobial
triples.object: An antimicrobial is an agent that kills microorganisms ( microbicide ) or stops their growth ( bacteriostatic agent ). [ 1 ] Antimicrobial medicines can be grouped according to the microorganisms they are used to treat. For example, antibiotics are used against bacteria , and antifungals are used ag

triples.subject: One Health Trust
British Society for Antimicrobial Chemotherapy
triples.object: The British Society for Antimicrobial Chemotherapy ( BSAC ) is a UK-based multi-professional organisation committed to preventing infectious diseases and tackling the growing threat of drug-resistant infections â one of the top global public health and development threats. [ 1 ]
